235	\subsection{Helper constructions underlying the group law}
236	\label{subsec:relpic_group_helpers}
237	
238	The abelian-group instance of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is assembled
239	from a handful of project-internal helper constructions on the locally-trivial
240	carrier \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T)\): the tensor-product operation that
241	realises addition, the local triviality of the unit (the zero element), the
242	uniqueness of tensor inverses (which makes negation well defined), and the two
243	descended operations on isomorphism classes. Each is recorded here for the
244	one-to-one Lean correspondence; all are proved directly in Lean.
245

247	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}

249	We now refine the set-valued functor of \texttt{pullback\_natural} into a
250	group-valued presheaf.

269	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
270	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
271	
272	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
273	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
274	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
275	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
276	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
277	the FGA Picard-representability chapter (parent scope), one must replace
278	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
279	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
280	main existence theorem in \S 4 represents.
281	
282	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
283	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf at b80f227 applies the
284	% sheafification machinery
285	% (\texttt{CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat}
286	% / \texttt{plusPlusSheaf}) to the constant-PUnit-functor placeholder
287	% supplied by \texttt{PicSharp.presheaf}; the sheafification machinery
288	% itself is correct, but the input presheaf is the wrong object, so
289	% \texttt{etSheaf} is the \'etale sheafification of a trivial constant
290	% functor rather than of the relative Picard presheaf. The closure is
291	% gated on the upstream Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules}
292	% monoidal-structure gap (it is precisely the upstream
293	% \texttt{PicSharp.presheaf} body that needs to be replaced). DO NOT
294	% promote this block to \leanok via the deterministic
295	% \texttt{sync\_leanok} phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in
296	% review, until the underlying \texttt{PicSharp} / \texttt{PicSharp.presheaf}
297	% bodies are replaced with the mathematically correct constructions.

8	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
9	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
10	
11	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
12	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
13	\texttt{rel\_pic\_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
14	\[
15	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
16	  \qquad
17	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
18	\]
19	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
20	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
21	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
22	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
23	
24	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
25	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
26	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
27	% groups)
28	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
29	% SOURCE QUOTE:
30	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
31	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
32	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
33	% abelian groups defined by the formula
34	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
35	% \end{dfn}
36	%  [\dots]
37	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
38	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
39	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
40	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
41	% topologies  by
42	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
43	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
44	%  \end{dfn}"
45	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
46	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
47	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
48	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
49	homomorphisms.}
50	
51	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
52	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
53	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
54	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
55	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
56	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
57	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
58	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
59	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
60	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
61	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
62	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
63	structure. This is the content of
64	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
65	
66	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
67	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
68	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
69	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
70	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
71	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
72	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
73	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
74	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
75	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
76	\begin{enumerate}
77	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
78	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
79	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
80	    isomorphism classes;
81	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
82	    for \(\otimes\);
83	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
84	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
85	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
86	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
87	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
88	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
89	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
90	\end{enumerate}
91	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
92	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
93	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
94	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
95	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
96	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
97	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
98	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
99	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
100	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
101	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
102	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
103	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
104	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
105	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
106	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
107	supplied.
108	
109	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
110	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
111	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
112	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
113	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
114	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
115	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
116	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
117	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
118	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
119	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
120	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
121	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
122	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
123	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
124	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
125	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
126	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
127	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
128	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
129	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
130	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
131	avoids. The
132	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
133	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
134	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
135	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
136	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
137	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
138	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
139	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
140	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
141	
142	\section{Mathlib API survey}
143	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}

148	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
149	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
150	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is

152	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
153	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
154	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib

249	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
250	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
251	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
252	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
253	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
254	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
255	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
256	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
257	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
258	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
259	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
260	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
261	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
262	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
263	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
264	the \emph{full} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or a coherent monoidal category.
265	The \emph{full monoidal-category} route~(e) is therefore off the critical path. The
266	associator does, however, consume a \emph{single} left-whisker existence lemma
267	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
268	commutation; so the stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) itself is on the critical path, even
269	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.

271	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
272	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
273	is already present in Mathlib: the file
274	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
275	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
276	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
277	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
278	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
279	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
280	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
281	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
282	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
283	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
284	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
285	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would
286	build is not pursued; but the (d.1)-linearity packaging and the (d.2) stalk--tensor
287	commutation are \emph{on} the critical path, since the associator's single open
288	left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} is closed
289	precisely by (d.2) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}). What is superseded is only the
290	\emph{pair}-of-whiskering-fields \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging and the
291	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.
292	
293	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
294	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
295	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
296	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
297	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
298	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
299	independently of any upstream timing.

73	  working template `pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp` (`analogies/d3cocycle006.md`); the older
74	  whiskerLeft/`d3-mate271.md` component approach is superseded (it kept hitting the dependent δ-splice).

81	  (0 overlap with both siblings).

83	## Mathlib gaps & new material
84	
85	Gaps to fill:
86	- DUAL route-2 `sliceDualTransport` — built by hand (leg-A slice-Hom base-change ∘
87	  leg-B unit ε-iso); ε-naturality via `PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxε`.
88	- A-bridge `homOfLocalCompat` — glues compatible local `𝒪_X`-module morphisms to a
89	  global morphism via `homLocalSection`, `topSectionToHom`, and `homMk`.
90	- D3′ — upgrade δ (`pullbackTensorMap`) to iso via `isIso_of_isIso_restrict`.
91	
92	New project material:
93	- By-hand `AddCommGroup` on loc-triv iso-classes (Mathlib `Sheaf.monoidalCategory`
94	  needs a FIXED `MonoidalCategory A`; varying-ring tensor on `X.Modules` has none).
95	  Modeled on Mathlib `CommRing.Pic.mapAlgebra`.

300	independently of any upstream timing.
301	
302	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
303	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
304	
305	\begin{definition}
306	
307	\leanok
308	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
309	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
311	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
312	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
313	  \[
314	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
315	  \]
316	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
317	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
318	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
319	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
320	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
321	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
322	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
323	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
324	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
325	  canonical compatibility
326	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
327	     \;\cong\;
328	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
329	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
330	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
331	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
332	
333	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
334	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
335	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
336	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
337	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
338	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
339	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
340	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{lemma}
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
347	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
351	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
353	  \[
354	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
357	  \]
358	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
359	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
360	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
361	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
362	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
363	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
364	  \[
365	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
366	    \qquad
367	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
368	  \]
369	  the associator
370	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
371	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
372	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
373	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
374	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
379	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
380	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
381	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
382	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
383	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
384	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
385	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
386	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
387	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
388	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
389	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
390	\end{proof}
391	
392	\begin{remark}
393	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
394	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:whisker_of_W}
396	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
397	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
398	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
399	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
400	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
401	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
402	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
403	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
404	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
405	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
406	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
407	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
408	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
409	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
410	
411	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
412	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
413	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
414	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
415	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
416	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
417	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
418	  fixed-base presheaves in
419	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
420	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
421	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \texttt{flat\_whisker\_localizer} (which is
422	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
423	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
424	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
425	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
426	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
427	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
428	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
429	
430	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
431	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
432	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
433	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
434	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
435	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
436	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
437	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
438	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
439	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
440	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
441	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
442	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
443	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
444	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
445	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
446	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
447	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
448	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
449	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
450	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
451	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
452	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
453	\end{remark}
454	
455	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
456	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
457	
458	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
459	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
460	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
461	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
462	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
463	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
464	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
465	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
466	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
467	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
468	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
469	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
470	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
471	\texttt{jw\_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
472	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
473	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
474	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
475	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
476	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
477	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
478	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
479	to the off-path route~(e).
480	
481	\begin{lemma}
482	
483	\leanok
484	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
485	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
486	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
487	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
488	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
489	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
490	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
491	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
492	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
493	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
494	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
495	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
496	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
497	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
498	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
499	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
500	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
501	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
502	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
503	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
504	  \[
505	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
506	      \;\;:=\;\;
507	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
508	  \]
509	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
510	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
511	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
512	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
513	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
514	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
515	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
516	\end{lemma}
517	
518	\begin{proof}
519	
520	\leanok
521	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map

528	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,

555	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}

557	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
558	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
559	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
560	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
561	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
562	  morphism
563	  \[
564	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
565	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
566	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
567	  \]
568	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
569	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
570	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
571	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
572	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
573	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
574	\end{lemma}

579	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
580	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
581	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
582	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
583	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
584	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
585	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image

587	  isomorphism}

589	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
590	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
591	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
592	  therefore extension of scalars along
593	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
594	
595	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
596	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
597	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
598	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
599	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
600	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
601	  the abstract pullback functor.

617	      \;\otimes\;

619	  \]

4657	\begin{lemma}[Leg-(B) unit \(\varepsilon\) is invertible, \(\mathrm{inv}\)-direction]

4843	  \mathtt{restr}_V\,\mathbf 1_Y\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\) is sent to an \(X\)-slice dual
4844	  section \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M.\mathtt{val} \to \mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,\mathbf 1_X\) over
4845	  \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\), where \(fV = f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(V)\). This is the inverse leg
4846	  of the forward equivalence \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}.

4848	\begin{proof}
4849	  \uses{def:dual_unit_ring_swap_hom, lem:image_preimage_of_le, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso_hom, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, def:presheaf_dual_unit_iso}
4850	  We build the underlying \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Hom}\) component-by-component over the
4851	  \(X\)-slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\), mirroring the forward component formula of
4852	  \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport} with two changes.
4853	
4854	  \emph{The component at \(W''\).} Fix \(W'' \le fV\) and set \(W' := W''.\mathrm{left}\) and
4855	  \(P := f^{-1}(W')\) for its preimage. Because \(f\) is an open immersion and
4856	  \(fV \subseteq \mathrm{range}\,f\), the open \(W' \le fV\) lies in the image of
4857	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\), so the down-set identity
4858	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(P) = W'\) holds --- but only \emph{propositionally}
4859	  (\cref{lem:image_preimage_of_le}); this is the single set-theoretic fact the inverse
4860	  reindexing depends on. The component of the reverse section at \(W''\) is the \(X\)-slice
4861	  mirror of the forward component:
4862	  \[
4863	    M.\mathtt{val}(\mathtt{eqToHom})
4864	      \;\circ\;
4865	    (\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,P).\mathrm{hom}).\mathrm{map}
4866	      \bigl(\psi_{(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(P \le V))}\bigr)
4867	      \;\circ\;
4868	    \mathtt{dualUnitRingSwapHom}\,f\,P,

4897	  \label{lem:dual_restrict_iso}
4898	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_restrict_iso}
4899	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, lem:slice_dual_transport}
4900	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
4901	  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
4902	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
4903	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4904	  % "Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a morphism
4905	  % of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules.
4906	  % If $\mathcal{F}$ is finitely presented and $f$ is flat,
4907	  % then the canonical map
4908	  % $$
4909	  % f^*\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
4910	  % \longrightarrow
4911	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(f^*\mathcal{F}, f^*\mathcal{G})
4912	  % $$
4913	  % is an isomorphism."
4914	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
4915	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
4916	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
4917	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
4918	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
4919	  \[
4920	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
4921	  \]
4922	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
4923	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
4924	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
4925	\end{lemma}
4926	
4927	\begin{proof}
4928	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, lem:slice_dual_transport}
4929	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
4930	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
4931	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
4932	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
4933	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
4934	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
4935	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
4936	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
4937	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
4938	
4939	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
4940	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
4941	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
4942	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
4943	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
4944	  \[
4945	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
4946	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
4947	  \]
4948	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
4949	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
4950	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
4951	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
4952	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
4953	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
4954	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
4955	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
4956	
4957	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
4958	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
4959	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
4960	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
4961	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
4962	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left
4963	  adjoints --- the adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) composed with
4964	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
4965	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
4966	  \emph{exactly}
4967	  \[
4968	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
4969	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
4970	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
4971	  \]
4972	  i.e.\ ``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual''. It is this residual ---
4973	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
4974	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
4975	  two-leg split of the whole step.
4976	
4977	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
4978	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
4979	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
4980	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain
4981	  \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) to the
4982	  restricted domain \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV) \subseteq
4983	  \mathrm{Opens}\,X\), and hence identifies the two Hom-modules computed over these matched
4984	  slices. This is a genuine slice-site Hom base-change --- a Beck--Chevalley square on the
4985	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set
4986	  of \(fV\) --- \emph{not} a fibrewise identity.
4987	
4988	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
4989	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
4990	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
4991	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
4992	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
4993	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
4994	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
4995	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
4996	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
4997	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\texttt{restrictscalars\_ringiso\_strongmonoidal}).
4998	
4999	  \emph{The combined leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) atom \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\).} Both legs are
5000	  realised together by a single \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism
5001	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) (\cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}), built
5002	  \emph{by hand} --- it is \emph{not} obtained by consuming a sheaf-level slice equivalence.
5003	  This is essential: the equivalence of \emph{sheaves of modules} over an open immersion
5004	  compares the restriction functors and the units, but carries \emph{no} internal-hom or
5005	  dual content; the assertion realized by leg (A) --- that the dual commutes with slice
5006	  reindexing along the open-immersion direct-image functor \(f_{!}\) on opens
5007	  (\(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\)) --- cannot be supplied by such an object without a
5008	  monoidal-closed structure on sheaves of modules, which this development deliberately does
5009	  \emph{not} carry (cf.\ \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The atom must
5010	  therefore be constructed directly.
5011	
5012	  Concretely (\cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}), the forward component of
5013	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) at \(W \le V\) is the categorical image
5014	  \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\,(\varphi_{\bullet})\) of the dual-section
5015	  component of \(\varphi\) at the \(f\)-image of \(W\), reindexed across
5016	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) by restriction of scalars along \(\beta_W\), post-composed with
5017	  the leg-(B) codomain swap \(\mathtt{inv}(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W))\).
5018	  Leg (A) is built categorically via \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\):
5019	  a genuine change-of-rings functor acts here, so the reindexing must be applied as a
5020	  functor map rather than pointwise. Leg (B)'s unit comparison \(\varepsilon\) is invertible
5021	  because \(\beta_W\) is a ring isomorphism, as detailed in \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}.
5022	  Because \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) is a thin poset, the
5023	  naturality squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The forward and inverse maps
5024	  identify the slice-Hom value over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\) with the slice-Hom value over
5025	  \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearly.
5026	
5027	  The Step-4 residual is then \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\) applied \emph{directly} to
5028	  the family \(V \mapsto \mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) --- which already packages
5029	  \emph{both} legs (A) and (B) --- with the outer naturality square thin-poset-trivial. No
5030	  separate leg-(B) step is interposed in \(\mathtt{isoMk}\): the per-section atom of
5031	  \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport} carries the full leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) content. The outer
5032	  \(\mathtt{isoMk}\) naturality square follows from the per-section family naturality
5033	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport.naturality}\) of \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}: the
5034	  \(\varepsilon\)-naturality is established first at the level of individual sections, and the
5035	  outer presheaf-morphism naturality of \(\mathtt{isoMk}\) then follows.
5036	
5037	  The residual of stage~H1 (``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual'') is
5038	  the sectionwise composite of leg (A) followed by leg (B), packaged over the slice with
5039	  poset-thin naturality; the full Step-4 isomorphism is then the H1 isomorphism followed by
5040	  this composite. The slice-reindexing coherence is \emph{light}, because \(\mathrm{Opens}\)
5041	  is a thin poset and the relevant squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
5042	  whereas H1 and legs (A) and (B) carry the substantive content.
5043	
5044	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
5045	  (\texttt{presheaf\_pushforward\_adj\_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For
5046	  the tensor product the transport \emph{collapsed to leg (B) alone}: because \(\otimes\)
5047	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single
5048	  ground-ring tensor equivalence, with no domain slice left to re-index. The dual's
5049	  non-sectionwise nature removes that collapse and forces the additional leg (A): the
5050	  domain \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) genuinely lives over a varying slice and must be
5051	  transported before the ground ring can be reconciled.
5052	
5053	  We caution that the naive ``fixed-value-category slice equivalence'' of
5054	  \texttt{open\_immersion\_slice\_sheaf\_equiv} is \emph{not} applicable to leg (A). That instrument
5055	  operates in a fixed value category over a fixed base ring, whereas the residual of
5056	  leg (A) lives at the \emph{presheaf} level, with a per-section ring \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)
5057	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
5058	  leg (A) must therefore be built directly at the presheaf-of-modules / varying-ring level.
5059	
5060	  Composing legs (A) and (B) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
5061	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values; assembling these into a
5062	  presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism and sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of
5063	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} resolves the H1 residual. Precomposing with the stage-H1
5064	  rewrite then yields the stated isomorphism
5065	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
5066	
5067	  \emph{The inverse-uniqueness shortcut is not viable.} One might hope to derive the
5068	  isomorphism from the already-established \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} by uniqueness of
5069	  monoidal inverses --- the dual being the \(\otimes\)-inverse of an invertible module and
5070	  restriction commuting with \(\otimes\) --- sidestepping leg (A). The abstract statements of
5071	  this idiom (\(\mathtt{hasRightDualOfEquivalence}\), \(\mathtt{rightDualIso}\)) require,
5072	  however, four structures the present setting lacks: a registered \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\)
5073	  instance on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) (the monoidal structure here is carried entirely
5074	  by hand), a \emph{strong} (not merely lax/oplax) monoidal restriction functor, restriction
5075	  being an \emph{equivalence} (false for a non-surjective open immersion, which is a
5076	  localization), and an exact pairing \(\mathtt{ExactPairing}\,M\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M)\) (which
5077	  exists only for dualizable \(M\), whereas the lemma is stated for general \(M\)). Even
5078	  restricted to the invertible consumer this would require building a coevaluation and
5079	  verifying the zig-zag identities --- strictly \emph{more} infrastructure than leg (A) --- so
5080	  the construction commits to legs (A) and (B) above.
5081	\end{proof}
5082	
5083	\begin{lemma}[Dual of the structure sheaf]
5084	  \label{lem:dual_unit_iso}
5085	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_unit_iso}
5086	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
5087	  Let \(Y\) be a scheme. The sheaf-level dual of the structure sheaf is canonically the
5088	  structure sheaf itself,
5089	  \[
5090	    \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
5091	  \]
5092	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf-level ``evaluation at \(1\)'' isomorphism
5093	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\): every endomorphism of the
5094	  unit presheaf is multiplication by its value at the global section \(1\), so evaluation
5095	  at \(1\) identifies the internal hom of the unit into itself with the unit.
5096	\end{lemma}
5097	
5098	\begin{proof}
5099	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, def:presheaf_dual_unit_iso}
5100	  At the presheaf level the dual of the unit \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the internal
5101	  hom \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1})\), whose value on an open \(U\) is the module
5102	  of \(\mathcal{O}(U)\)-linear endomorphisms of \(\mathbf{1}|_U\). Each such endomorphism is
5103	  determined by its value at the global section \(1\) and equals multiplication by that
5104	  value; hence \emph{evaluation at \(1\)} is itself the sectionwise isomorphism
5105	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\) of presheaves of modules, with
5106	  inverse the scalar-action map \(r \mapsto (\,\cdot\,r)\). (No left unitor is needed: the
5107	  identification is the direct evaluation-at-\(1\) / global-scalar-multiplication
5108	  isomorphism, not the composite through \(\mathbf{1} \otimes \mathbf{1} \cong \mathbf{1}\).)
5109	  Sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} yields
5110	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\). The scheme-level alias of this
5111	  presheaf evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism is
5112	  \(\mathtt{presheafDualUnitIso}\) (\cref{def:presheaf_dual_unit_iso}).
5113	\end{proof}
5114	
5115	\begin{lemma}
5116	
5117	[The dual of a line bundle is a line bundle]
5118	  \label{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
5119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_isLocallyTrivial}
5120	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso, lem:dual_unit_iso, def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5121	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
5122	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2)
5123	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213).
5124	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5125	  % "\item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
5126	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
5127	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
5128	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
5129	  % is an isomorphism."
5130	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR,
5131	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item~2).}
5132	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
5133	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
5134	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of
5135	  rank one, i.e.\ \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,L)\).
5136	\end{lemma}
5137	
5138	\begin{proof}
5139	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso, lem:dual_unit_iso, def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5140	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an affine open \(U\) with a
5141	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\); writing \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) for the
5142	  associated open immersion, it suffices to exhibit an isomorphism
5143	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). This is the following three-step chain:
5144	  \[
5145	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f
5146	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
5147	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U
5148	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_U.
5149	  \]
5150	  \begin{enumerate}
5151	    \item The first isomorphism is the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
5152	      \cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}: restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes
5153	      with the formation of the dual. This is the C-bridge proper; its substantive content
5154	      is the sectionwise ring-iso transport of the module structure
5155	      (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}), as detailed there.
5156	    \item The second isomorphism is the dual applied to the trivialisation
5157	      \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), via the contravariant functoriality of the sheaf-level
5158	      dual in isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\),
5159	      \cref{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}): an isomorphism \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\)
5160	      induces \(\mathtt{dual}(L|_U) \cong \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U\).
5161	    \item The third isomorphism trivialises the dual of the unit,
5162	      \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (\cref{lem:dual_unit_iso}):
5163	      the dual of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf itself, the comparison being
5164	      evaluation at \(1 \in \mathcal{O}_U\), under which
5165	      \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U) \cong \mathcal{O}_U\).
5166	  \end{enumerate}
5167	  Composing the three exhibits \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). Hence
5168	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
5169	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).
5170	\end{proof}
5171	
5172	\begin{lemma}
5173	
5174	\leanok
5175	[Local isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global isomorphism]
5176	  \label{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}
5177	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_restrict}
5178	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi \colon M \to N\) a morphism in
5179	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\).
